Analyzing EPA PM2.5 Data

Example: San Bernardino County

Marc Los Huertos

Spring 2026

Updated: 2026-03-07 22:34:50

1 Introduction

Particulate matter smaller than 2.5 micrometers in diameter (PM2.5) is among the most health-relevant air pollutants measured by the EPA. Because these particles are small enough to penetrate deep into the lungs and enter the bloodstream, chronic or acute exposure is associated with respiratory disease, cardiovascular disease, and premature mortality (Pope and Dockery 2006Pope, C. Arden, and Douglas W. Dockery. 2006. “Health Effects of Fine Particulate Air Pollution: Lines That Connect.” Journal of the Air & Waste Management Association 56 (6): 709–42. https://doi.org/10.1080/10473289.2006.10464485.). Concentrations vary substantially across space and time, driven by local sources (traffic, industry, agriculture, wildfires) as well as regional meteorology such as temperature inversions that trap pollutants near the surface.

In Southern California, and San Bernardino County in particular, PM2.5 levels are shaped by a combination of factors: heavy diesel truck traffic along Interstate 10 and 15, inland warehousing logistics, agricultural burning, and episodic wildfire smoke. Seasonal patterns are pronounced — summer and fall months often see elevated concentrations due to both increased wildfire activity and stable high-pressure systems that limit vertical mixing (Jaffe et al. 2008Jaffe, Daniel, William Hafner, Duli Chand, Anthony Westerling, and Dominick Spracklen. 2008. “Interannual Variations in PM2.5 Due to Wildfires in the Western United States.” Environmental Science & Technology 42 (8): 2812–18. https://doi.org/10.1021/es702755v.).

1.1 Goals

This exercise has two goals. First, we characterize ten years of EPA PM2.5 data for San Bernardino County to understand baseline concentrations, seasonal patterns, and station-to-station variability. Second, we compare measurements from our own low-cost Raspberry Pi sensor against the EPA record to evaluate whether our sensor produces values consistent with the established monitoring network.

1.2 Regulatory Standards

The EPA has established two National Ambient Air Quality Standards (NAAQS) for PM2.5:

  • 24-hour standard: 35 µg/m³ — No more than 1 exceedance per year is permitted on average. This standard targets acute, short-term exposure events such as wildfire smoke episodes.
  • Annual standard: 12 µg/m³ — The annual mean concentration averaged over three years must not exceed this level. This standard targets chronic exposure affecting long-term health.

California has an additional, more protective annual standard of 12 µg/m³ and a 24-hour standard of 35 µg/m³, consistent with federal levels but enforced independently by the California Air Resources Board (CARB). When we plot thresholds below, we use 35 µg/m³ as the 24-hour reference line because our data are daily values.

1.3 Learning Outcomes

By completing this assignment you will be able to:

  1. Retrieve, load, and clean EPA air quality CSV data in R.
  2. Compute summary statistics (median, mean, SD, 95% CI) grouped by site and month.
  3. Produce publication-quality time-series plots with regulatory threshold annotations.
  4. Conduct a one-sample and two-sample t-test to evaluate whether sensor data are statistically consistent with EPA observations.
  5. Interpret confidence intervals and p-values in an environmental health context.

1.4 Grading (10 points)

Criterion Points
10 years of PM2.5 AQI downloaded and loaded correctly 2
Summary statistics table (median, mean, SD, 95% CI) per station 2
Scatter plot with rotated axis labels, proper axis labels, EPA threshold line, and monthly CI estimates 3
Discussion (2–8 peer-reviewed sources, 2–4 paragraphs on regional causes and implications) 3

Submit both the .Rmd source file and the knitted .pdf via Canvas.

2 Procedures

2.1 Accessing EPA Data via the AQS API

Rather than downloading data manually, we retrieve it programmatically using the RAQSAPI package, which wraps the EPA’s Air Quality System (AQS) Data Mart API. This makes the analysis fully reproducible — anyone who knits this document gets the same data without manual steps.

2.1.1 Download and Cache 10 Years of Daily PM2.5

The API enforces a rate limit of 10 requests per minute and limits each request to one calendar year, so a 10-year pull makes 10 sequential calls with a built-in 5-second pause between each. To avoid re-downloading every time you knit, we cache the result as a local RDS file.

library(dplyr)
library(lubridate)

# --- Parameters you might change -------------------------------------------
county      <- "San Bernardino"
state_fips  <- "06"    # California FIPS
county_fips <- "071"   # San Bernardino County FIPS
end_year    <- as.integer(format(Sys.Date(), "%Y")) - 1   # last complete year
start_year  <- end_year - 9                               # 10 years total
cache_file  <- "data/SB_PM25_daily.rds"
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------

# The EPA publishes complete national daily PM2.5 CSV files at a stable URL.
# No account or API key required. Each annual file is ~50 MB zipped and is
# filtered to the target county after download.
# First run takes a few minutes; results are cached so subsequent knits are instant.

base_url <- "https://aqs.epa.gov/aqsweb/airdata/daily_88101_%d.zip"

if (!dir.exists("data")) dir.create("data")

if (file.exists(cache_file)) {
  message("Loading cached data from: ", cache_file)
  SB_raw <- readRDS(cache_file)

} else {
  years   <- seq(start_year, end_year)
  SB_list <- vector("list", length(years))

  for (i in seq_along(years)) {
    yr  <- years[i]
    url <- sprintf(base_url, yr)
    tmp <- tempfile(fileext = ".zip")

    message("Downloading ", yr, " (", i, "/", length(years), ")...")
    tryCatch({
      download.file(url, tmp, quiet = TRUE, mode = "wb")
      csv <- unzip(tmp, exdir = tempdir())
      df  <- read.csv(csv[1], stringsAsFactors = FALSE)

      # Filter to target county
      df <- df[df$State.Code  == as.integer(state_fips) &
               df$County.Code == as.integer(county_fips), ]

      SB_list[[i]] <- df
      unlink(tmp); unlink(csv)
    }, error = function(e) {
      message("  Skipping ", yr, ": ", conditionMessage(e))
    })
  }

  SB_raw <- do.call(rbind, SB_list)
  saveRDS(SB_raw, cache_file)
  message("Done. Cache saved to: ", cache_file)
}

cat("Rows:", nrow(SB_raw), "| Years:", start_year, "-", end_year, "
")
## Rows: 28602 | Years: 2016 - 2025

2.1.2 Prepare the Working Dataframe

The API returns many columns. We select and rename the ones we need, then build a Site.ID string that matches the familiar SS-CCC-NNNN format used in EPA reports.

# The bulk CSV uses different column names than the API.
# Key columns: State.Code, County.Code, Site.Num, Local.Site.Name,
#              Date.Local, AQI, Arithmetic.Mean
SB <- SB_raw %>%
  transmute(
    Site.ID         = paste0(
                        formatC(State.Code,  width = 2, flag = "0"), "-",
                        formatC(County.Code, width = 3, flag = "0"), "-",
                        formatC(Site.Num,    width = 4, flag = "0")),
    Site.Name       = Local.Site.Name,
    Date            = as.Date(Date.Local),
    PM2.5.AQI.Value = AQI,
    PM2.5.Conc      = Arithmetic.Mean   # µg/m³
  ) %>%
  filter(!is.na(PM2.5.AQI.Value))

str(SB)
## 'data.frame':    17204 obs. of  5 variables:
##  $ Site.ID        : chr  "06-071-0027" "06-071-0027" "06-071-0027" "06-071-0027" ...
##  $ Site.Name      : chr  "Ontario-Route 60 Near Road" "Ontario-Route 60 Near Road" "Ontario-Route 60 Near Road" "Ontario-Route 60 Near Road" ...
##  $ Date           : Date, format: "2016-01-01" "2016-01-02" ...
##  $ PM2.5.AQI.Value: int  80 65 69 62 56 52 52 68 63 70 ...
##  $ PM2.5.Conc     : num  24.4 16.6 19 14.8 11.9 9.9 9.4 18.1 15.4 19.1 ...

2.1.3 Remove Collocated QA Monitors

Two stations (06-071-9010 and 06-071-9002) are collocated quality-assurance monitors, not primary network stations. We remove them to avoid double-counting.

SB <- subset(SB, !(Site.ID %in% c("06-071-9010", "06-071-9002")))

2.1.4 Remove Low-Sampling Stations

Stations with fewer than 100 observations across the 10-year record provide insufficient data for reliable monthly estimates. We calculate observation counts per station and drop those below the threshold.

SB.N <- aggregate(PM2.5.AQI.Value ~ Site.ID, data = SB, FUN = length)
names(SB.N)[2] <- "N"
print(SB.N)
##       Site.ID    N
## 1 06-071-0027 6513
## 2 06-071-0306 6770
## 3 06-071-2002 1109
## 4 06-071-8001 1724
## 5 06-071-9004 1088
keep_ids <- SB.N$Site.ID[SB.N$N >= 100]
SB <- subset(SB, Site.ID %in% keep_ids)
cat("Stations retained:", length(unique(SB$Site.ID)), "\n")
## Stations retained: 5

3 Exploratory Graphic Analysis

Before computing statistics, it is good practice to visualize the raw data to identify outliers, gaps in the record, or stations that behave unusually.

3.1 Box Plot by Station

A box plot lets us compare the distributions across stations simultaneously. Stations with very high medians or many outliers warrant investigation.

par(las = 2, mar = c(8, 4, 3, 1))
boxplot(PM2.5.AQI.Value ~ Site.ID, data = SB,
        main = paste("PM2.5 by Station —", county),
        ylab = "PM2.5 AQI (µg/m³)", xlab = "",
        ylim = c(0, 200), col = "lightblue")
abline(h = 35, col = "red", lty = 2, lwd = 1.5)
legend("topright", legend = "24-hr NAAQS (35 µg/m³)",
       col = "red", lty = 2, bty = "n")

Distribution of daily PM2.5 AQI by monitoring station. Horizontal line at 35 marks the 24-hour NAAQS standard.

3.2 Station Names and Median Ranking

SB.unique  <- unique(SB[c("Site.ID", "Site.Name")])
SB.median  <- aggregate(PM2.5.AQI.Value ~ Site.ID, data = SB, FUN = median)
SB.median  <- merge(SB.median, SB.unique, by = "Site.ID")
SB.median  <- SB.median[order(SB.median$PM2.5.AQI.Value), ]
print(SB.median)
##       Site.ID PM2.5.AQI.Value                  Site.Name
## 4 06-071-8001              29                   Big Bear
## 2 06-071-0306              46    Victorville-Park Avenue
## 5 06-071-9004              54             San Bernardino
## 3 06-071-2002              55                    Fontana
## 1 06-071-0027              59 Ontario-Route 60 Near Road

Stations are now ranked from lowest to highest median PM2.5. This helps prioritize which sites to focus on in the discussion.

3.3 Time-Series: All Stations

par(las = 1)
plot(PM2.5.AQI.Value ~ Date, data = SB,
     xlab = "Date", ylab = "PM2.5 AQI (µg/m³)",
     main = paste("PM2.5 —", county, "(All Stations)"),
     pch = 20, cex = 0.4, col = rgb(0, 0, 0, 0.25))
abline(h = 35, col = "red", lty = 2)

Daily PM2.5 AQI for all retained stations in San Bernardino County. Red dashed line = 24-hr NAAQS.

3.4 Time-Series by Station

site_ids <- unique(SB$Site.ID)
n_sites  <- length(site_ids)
ncols    <- 3
nrows    <- ceiling(n_sites / ncols)
colors   <- rainbow(n_sites)

par(las = 1, mfrow = c(nrows, ncols), mar = c(3, 4, 3, 1))
for (i in seq_along(site_ids)) {
  sub <- subset(SB, Site.ID == site_ids[i])
  site_label <- SB.unique$Site.Name[SB.unique$Site.ID == site_ids[i]]
  plot(PM2.5.AQI.Value ~ Date, data = sub,
       xlab = "", ylab = "PM2.5 (µg/m³)",
       main = site_label,
       pch = 20, cex = 0.5, col = colors[i],
       ylim = c(0, 200))
  abline(h = 35, col = "red", lty = 2)
}
par(mfrow = c(1, 1))

Daily PM2.5 AQI per station. Red dashed line = 24-hr NAAQS (35 µg/m³).

4 Monthly Summary Statistics

To understand seasonal patterns, we compute monthly summary statistics across all years. We use dplyr for grouped summaries and lubridate for date parsing.

library(dplyr)
library(lubridate)

Monthly <- SB %>%
  mutate(Date  = ymd(Date),
         Year  = year(Date),
         Month = month(Date)) %>%
  group_by(Month, Site.ID) %>%
  summarise(
    median = median(PM2.5.AQI.Value, na.rm = TRUE),
    mean   = mean(PM2.5.AQI.Value,   na.rm = TRUE),
    sd     = sd(PM2.5.AQI.Value,     na.rm = TRUE),
    N      = n(),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

head(Monthly)
## # A tibble: 6 × 6
##   Month Site.ID     median  mean    sd     N
##   <dbl> <chr>        <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <int>
## 1     1 06-071-0027   60    61.5  22.3   360
## 2     1 06-071-0306   37    36.4  17.2   341
## 3     1 06-071-2002   52    49.0  24.6    63
## 4     1 06-071-8001   34.5  34.9  18.2    80
## 5     1 06-071-9004   52    49.3  26.1    61
## 6     2 06-071-0027   55.5  55.5  16.8   336

4.1 Confidence Intervals

The sample mean \(\bar{x}\) is an estimate of the true population mean. Because it is estimated from a finite sample, it carries uncertainty. The 95% confidence interval quantifies that uncertainty: if we repeated the sampling process many times, 95% of the resulting intervals would contain the true mean. Practically, it tells us the range of values that are plausible for the long-run average given the data we have.

\[CI_{95} = \bar{x} \pm 1.96 \times \frac{s}{\sqrt{N}}\]

Where \(s\) is the sample standard deviation and \(N\) is the number of observations. We use \(z = 1.96\) because for large \(N\) the sampling distribution of the mean is approximately normal.

Monthly$UCL95 <- Monthly$mean + qnorm(0.975) * Monthly$sd / sqrt(Monthly$N)
Monthly$LCL95 <- Monthly$mean - qnorm(0.975) * Monthly$sd / sqrt(Monthly$N)

4.2 Monthly Summary Table

library(xtable)

# Show one representative station for readability
focus_site <- SB.median$Site.ID[ceiling(nrow(SB.median) / 2)]   # median-ranked station

tbl <- Monthly[Monthly$Site.ID == focus_site, ]
tbl$Month <- month.abb[tbl$Month]   # convert 1–12 to Jan–Dec
tbl <- tbl[, c("Month", "N", "median", "mean", "sd", "LCL95", "UCL95")]

print(
  xtable(tbl,
         caption = paste("Monthly PM2.5 AQI summary for station", focus_site),
         digits  = c(0, 0, 0, 1, 1, 1, 1, 1)),
  include.rownames = FALSE,
  type = "html",
  caption.placement = "top"
)
Monthly PM2.5 AQI summary for station 06-071-9004
Month N median mean sd LCL95 UCL95
Jan 61 52.0 49.3 26.1 42.7 55.8
Feb 47 33.0 38.5 18.9 33.1 43.9
Mar 50 43.0 42.2 16.8 37.5 46.8
Apr 54 47.5 47.0 14.1 43.2 50.7
May 62 53.0 49.6 12.8 46.5 52.8
Jun 58 57.0 56.6 7.8 54.6 58.6
Jul 47 58.0 63.3 22.4 56.9 69.7
Aug 52 57.5 58.5 5.7 57.0 60.0
Sep 49 54.0 57.0 17.9 52.0 62.0
Oct 43 53.0 51.8 14.9 47.4 56.3
Nov 46 52.0 52.0 19.3 46.4 57.6
Dec 47 54.0 56.1 24.8 49.0 63.2
472 54.0 51.6 17.5 50.0 53.2

4.3 Monthly Mean ± 95% CI Plot

focus <- Monthly[Monthly$Site.ID == focus_site, ]
focus <- focus[order(focus$Month), ]

par(las = 1)
plot(focus$Month, focus$mean,
     type = "b", pch = 16,
     xlim = c(0.5, 12.5), ylim = c(0, max(focus$UCL95, 35) * 1.1),
     xaxt = "n",
     xlab = "Month", ylab = "Mean PM2.5 AQI (µg/m³)",
     main = paste("Monthly PM2.5 Mean ± 95% CI\n", focus_site))
axis(1, at = 1:12, labels = month.abb)
arrows(focus$Month, focus$LCL95,
       focus$Month, focus$UCL95,
       angle = 90, code = 3, length = 0.05)
abline(h = 35, col = "red", lty = 2)
legend("topleft", legend = "24-hr NAAQS (35 µg/m³)",
       col = "red", lty = 2, bty = "n")

Monthly mean PM2.5 AQI ± 95% CI. Error bars reflect uncertainty in the mean, not the full spread of daily values. Red dashed line = 24-hr NAAQS.

5 Raspberry Pi Sensor Analysis

5.1 Load the Data

Download your group’s CSV from Canvas and place it in the data/ folder, naming it RPi_sensor.csv. The timestamp column should be in ISO 8601 format (YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS). The PM2.5 concentration column should be named PM2.5.

If the file is not yet present, all chunks in this section are automatically skipped so the document still knits cleanly.

rpi_file   <- "data/RPi_sensor.csv"
rpi_exists <- file.exists(rpi_file)
if (!rpi_exists) message("RPi data not found at ", rpi_file, " -- skipping sensor sections.")

5.2 Parse Timestamps and Summarize

RPi          <- read.csv(rpi_file)
RPi$Datetime <- ymd_hms(RPi$Date)
RPi$Month    <- month(RPi$Datetime)
RPi$Day      <- day(RPi$Datetime)
RPi$Hour     <- hour(RPi$Datetime)
head(RPi)
RPi.summary <- RPi %>%
  summarise(
    N      = n(),
    median = median(PM2.5, na.rm = TRUE),
    mean   = mean(PM2.5,   na.rm = TRUE),
    sd     = sd(PM2.5,     na.rm = TRUE)
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    UCL95 = mean + qnorm(0.975) * sd / sqrt(N),
    LCL95 = mean - qnorm(0.975) * sd / sqrt(N)
  )

print(RPi.summary)

6 Hypothesis Testing: Is Our Sensor Consistent with EPA Data?

We now test whether our Raspberry Pi sensor readings are statistically consistent with the EPA monitoring record for the same period. We use two complementary tests.

6.1 Approach 1 — One-Sample t-test Against EPA Monthly Mean

The EPA monthly mean for the month our sensor was deployed gives us an expected value \(\mu_0\). Our null hypothesis is:

\[H_0: \mu_{sensor} = \mu_{EPA}\]

If the p-value is less than 0.05, we reject \(H_0\) and conclude our sensor readings differ systematically from the EPA reference.

# Pull the EPA mean for the deployment month (update month number as needed)
deployment_month <- month(RPi$Datetime[1])
epa_mean <- Monthly$mean[Monthly$Site.ID == focus_site &
                           Monthly$Month  == deployment_month]

cat("EPA reference mean for month", deployment_month, ":", round(epa_mean, 2), "µg/m³\n")

t_result <- t.test(RPi$PM2.5, mu = epa_mean)
print(t_result)

Interpreting the result: If the confidence interval printed above contains epa_mean, the sensor is statistically consistent with the EPA record at the 95% level. A low p-value (< 0.05) indicates a systematic bias that may reflect sensor calibration, placement (indoors vs. outdoors, proximity to local sources), or a genuine microenvironment difference rather than network-wide conditions.

6.2 Approach 2 — Two-Sample t-test: Sensor vs. EPA Daily Values

When we have many sensor readings rather than just a mean, a two-sample t-test is more appropriate. This test asks whether the two samples could plausibly come from populations with the same mean, without assuming we know what that mean is.

\[H_0: \mu_{sensor} = \mu_{EPA,\,daily}\]

# Extract EPA daily values for the same station and deployment month
epa_daily <- SB$PM2.5.AQI.Value[
  SB$Site.ID == focus_site &
  month(SB$Date) == deployment_month
]

t_result2 <- t.test(RPi$PM2.5, epa_daily)
print(t_result2)

Interpreting the result: A non-significant result (p > 0.05) supports the conclusion that our sensor and the EPA network are measuring comparable concentrations. A significant result prompts follow-up questions: Is the sensor co-located with the EPA station? Was the deployment period affected by a local event (e.g., a nearby fire, construction) that would not be captured by a distant EPA monitor?

7 Discussion

Replace this section with your own analysis. Below is a scaffold to guide you.

San Bernardino County consistently ranks among the most polluted air basins in the United States. Several factors drive this pattern:

Regional meteorology. The South Coast Air Basin sits in a bowl bounded by the San Gabriel and San Bernardino mountains. Thermally driven circulation patterns — particularly the land-sea breeze — transport pollutants from Los Angeles eastward into the Inland Empire, where they accumulate against the mountain barrier (Kleeman et al. 2007Kleeman, Michael J., Qi Ying, Jake Lu, Matthew J. Mysliwiec, Robert J. Griffin, Jianjun Chen, and Simon Clegg. 2007. “Source Apportionment of Secondary Organic Aerosol During a Severe Photochemical Smog Episode in the South Coast Air Basin of California.” Journal of Geophysical Research: Atmospheres 112: D03201. https://doi.org/10.1029/2006JD007400.). Temperature inversions are especially common in summer and fall, suppressing vertical mixing and concentrating surface-level PM2.5.

Wildfire smoke. California’s fire season has lengthened significantly over the past two decades. Smoke from fires burning in the Sierra Nevada and the Transverse Ranges can elevate PM2.5 across the Inland Empire by tens of µg/m³ for days at a time, producing the sharp spikes visible in our scatter plots (Liu et al. 2015Liu, Jia C., Gavin Pereira, Stephanie A. Uhl, Mercedes A. Bravo, and Michelle L. Bell. 2015. “A Systematic Review of the Physical Health Impacts from Non-Occupational Exposure to Wildfire Smoke.” Environmental Research 136: 120–32. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.envres.2014.10.015.).

Mobile and freight sources. The region hosts one of the highest concentrations of diesel freight activity in the country, centered on the ports of Los Angeles and Long Beach and the inland distribution warehouses that line Interstate 15 and State Route 60. Diesel combustion produces both direct PM2.5 emissions and precursor gases (NOx, SO2) that form secondary particulate matter (Zhu et al. 2002Zhu, Yifang, William C. Hinds, Sungho Kim, Sheng Shen, and Constantinos Sioutas. 2002. “Study of Ultrafine Particles Near a Major Highway with Heavy-Duty Diesel Traffic.” Atmospheric Environment 36 (27): 4323–35. https://doi.org/10.1016/S1352-2310(02)00354-0.).

Your discussion should: (1) describe the seasonal pattern visible in your monthly summary plot and explain the mechanism; (2) identify whether any 24-hr or annual standards were exceeded and over what proportion of days; (3) evaluate whether your sensor data are consistent with the EPA record and offer an interpretation if they are not; and (4) discuss one or two implications for environmental justice given the demographics of communities near the stations you analyzed.

sessionInfo()

References
